title: A ‘giant hole in consumer safety’: super test up for revamp
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Agribusiness A ‘giant hole in consumer safety’: super test up for revamp theaustralian.com.au Cliona O’Dowd 3 May 2026 04:05 GMT 828 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. A ‘modernised’ superannuation performance test could open up investment in nation-building assets but only if the returns stack up, the super industry has warned, as the Albanese government takes steps to boost fund investment in defence, infrastructure and housing. As funds prepares for yet another round of consultation with Treasury on the sector’s annual performance test, which is expected to kick off as soon as this week, it is the potential inclusion of platform and retirement products that is causing the most friction, with the industry welcoming the prospect of targeted changes to discourage the herd behaviour seen in investments over recent years. David Elia, chief executive of the $150bn hospitality industry fund Hostplus, said he supported tweaks to the current test, as he acknowledged it had pushed funds toward short-term benchmark hugging since it was brought in five years ago. “We’re encouraged that the government is consulting on the framework. Thoughtful refinements that maintain accountability, while better recognising long-term, diversified investment strategies, would be a positive step,” Mr Elia said. “Our focus is always on delivering strong long-term returns for members. That includes investing in sectors that drive innovation, productivity and economic growth areas that may not always be well captured by traditional benchmarks, but are critical to Australia’s future. “Ultimately, the objective should be alignment, a system that continues to protect members, while also enabling superannuation to play its full role as a long-term investor in the Australian economy,” he said. At the same time, the industry is warning it will not be pushed to invest in assets that don’t drive the best return outcome for members. “Funds’ obligation to invest in members’ best financial interests duty remains sacrosanct,” Financial Services Council chief executive Blake Briggs said. “Any changes must not water down the test by allowing underperforming MySuper products to escape scrutiny or for funds to increase their fees, such as through changes to how current year fees are assessed,” Mr Briggs said. The FSC, which supports limited changes to the test to ensure it is not limiting strategic investment, has proposed to Treasury that a CPI+X benchmark be used for asset classes that don’t fit neatly within existing benchmarks. While funds broadly support changes to disincentivise herd behaviour, it is the potential inclusion of retirement and platform products that is causing more friction within the industry. “You’ve got millions of consumers who are essentially flying blind, not knowing whether or not their super is meeting that performance benchmark that has been set for the rest of the mainstream super architecture,” Super Members Council chief executive Misha Schubert said of the exclusion of platform products. “It’s a giant hole in consumer safety,” she added. “The 12,000 Australians who lost their life savings in the Shield and First Guardian schemes, those products were not subject to the performance test and yet were available on four platforms and were allowed to receive compulsory tax-preferred super guarantee contributions,” she said. Shield and First Guardian were two managed investment schemes heavily exposed to property and private lending that were put into liquidation in 2024 and 2025 and are currently under investigation for allegedly misusing investor money. All up, close to $1bn of super money was invested in the two failed funds, which were available on platforms hosted by Macquarie, Netwealth, Equity Trustees and Diversa. While all MySuper products, the default options offered by funds, are included in the test, the ‘choice’ component of the test only assesses products offered by the trustees behind the platforms. External offerings and managed investment schemes on platforms are excluded. Ms Schubert said objections to including platform products in the performance test did not stand up to scrutiny, adding that it was crucial consumers had visibility of how their super was performing. But the FSC, which represents for-profit retail funds, pushed back against such a proposal, arguing against platform inclusion. “Expanding the test to platform products would be inherently complex and generate perverse outcomes, as has been seen in the test’s application to other choice options,” Mr Briggs said. “The methodology applied for MySuper products simply cannot take into account the diverse universe of choice options available and risks undermining the advice-supported decisions made by consumers,” he cautioned. The industry is also split on whether retirement products should be assessed in the test, with some arguing the retirement phase was not about maximising returns and rather about meeting more diverse needs such as access, steady income and longevity. “Retirement products are fundamentally different to accumulation products, and a single performance metric risks misrepresenting how they are designed to deliver a members’ bespoke retirement goals,” Mr Briggs warned. Ms Schubert said the SMC was open to the consideration of a “bespoke quality filter” in the test that captured the retirement phase. Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Albanese government is considering a revamp of the superannuation performance test to encourage investment in nation-building assets. Industry leaders support targeted changes to enhance long-term investment strategies while ensuring consumer safety, particularly regarding platform and retirement products. The debate highlights concerns over transparency and accountability in superannuation investments, especially following significant losses in certain managed investment schemes.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Proposed changes aim to modernise the superannuation performance test to include platform and retirement products.
Concerns exist about consumer safety and the need for transparency in superannuation performance.
Industry leaders advocate for a balance between accountability and the ability to invest in long-term growth sectors.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts investment strategies, regulatory compliance, and consumer protection in the superannuation sector.
